coredata.dc:description: Biopolymer based intelligent packaging films with barrier properties are gaining significant attention in the food industry due to the compostability, food freshness monitoring potential and shelf life increment capability. In this work, ∼10 nm sized urea- dithiosalicilic acid based carbon dots (UCDs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) property are prepared and incorporated into carrageenan biopolymer to develop amine sensing packaging material. Composite film is coated with zein-stearic acid (Z/SA) mixture to improve the water barrier property with water vapour transmission rate value ≤40 g/(m2.day). The coated composite film (CAR3UCD-C) exhibited ammonia sensing capability with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.6 μM and showed good free radical scavenging activity (70 % against ABTS radicals), high UV blocking property (∼92 % reduction) as well as antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus. The developed films showed better tensile strength (>50 MPa) and good thermal stability (till 200 °C). The material effectively distinguished the freshness states of prawns through fluorescent color change from orange to blue upon spoilage. The coated film is completely degradable in soil within 35 days under ambient conditions. This film can be utilised as a promising intelligent barrier packaging material for monitoring real-time freshness of prawns and other protein-rich foods.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Biopolymer based intelligent packaging films with barrier properties are gaining significant attention in the food industry due to the compostability, food freshness monitoring potential and shelf life increment capability. In this work, ∼10 nm sized urea- dithiosalicilic acid based carbon dots (UCDs) with aggregation-induced emission (AIE) property are prepared and incorporated into carrageenan biopolymer to develop amine sensing packaging material. Composite film is coated with zein-stearic acid (Z/SA) mixture to improve the water barrier property with water vapour transmission rate value ≤40 g/(m2.day). The coated composite film (CAR3UCD-C) exhibited ammonia sensing capability with a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.6 μM and showed good free radical scavenging activity (70 % against ABTS radicals), high UV blocking property (∼92 % reduction) as well as antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus. The developed films showed better tensile strength (>50 MPa) and good thermal stability (till 200 °C). The material effectively distinguished the freshness states of prawns through fluorescent color change from orange to blue upon spoilage. The coated film is completely degradable in soil within 35 days under ambient conditions. This film can be utilised as a promising intelligent barrier packaging material for monitoring real-time freshness of prawns and other protein-rich foods.

ce:section.ce:para: Spoilage of huge amount of food items occur due to the mishandling in packaging, distribution and delay in consumption are responsible for many foodborne diseases and pose a serious threat to public health safety and economy. In particular, meats and aquatic foods, the most consumed protein sources disintegrate rapidly under inappropriate storage conditions and produce biogenic amines (BAs) like trimethyl amine, ammonia etc. through microbial enzymatic processes and causes serious health issues [1–4]. Due to the increased demand for such proteinaceous food items, there is a necessity to develop a sustainable and biodegradable intelligent packaging material which can monitor the freshness status of the food through BA tracking [5,6].

Smart packaging materials with the ability to detect BAs are usually developed by incorporating suitable indicator into a polymer matrix used for packaging [1,7,8]. BAs sensing and detection using fluorescent techniques have grabbed wide attention over the past few decades due to the rapid responsive nature, high degree of sensitivity, stability and non-invasive testing manner [3,9]. Among them, carbon dots (CDs), a novel class of metal-free carbon-based fluorescent materials, have gained significant attention for food packaging applications recently due to their low toxicity, antibacterial and antioxidant properties, UV blocking property, biocompatibility, strong emission intensity, and compatibility with polymer matrices [10–12]. CDs with pH and amine sensing properties are reported to track changes associated with food spoilage [10,13,14]. Zhang et al. synthesised red emissive CDs from citric acid and urea through solvothermal process and showcased its emission intensity enhancement in exposure to alkaline organic amines and BAs derived from spoiled shrimp to achieve food spoilage detection [13]. CDs based ratiometric fluorescent tags with self-calibration capacity have emerged later on in this field in order to tackle the possible sensitivity issues associated with the systems that rely on single fluorescence peak intensity. Lin et al. fabricated such a system by mixing two different types of CDs with blue and red emissions derived from poplar saw dust lignin and pyromellitic acid respectively, where the probe in liquid phase exhibited a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.045 μM towards BAs [4]. Afterwards, a new kind of CDs with dual emission property was introduced to effectively eliminate tedious making process of these two components containing systems. Song et al. prepared a ratiometric trimethyl amine (TMA) sensor based on p-phenylene diamine derived CDs loaded electrospun poly vinylidene fluoride (PVDF) film (1.04 μM LOD towards TMA) which detected the volatile amines from beef, pork and shrimp in a packaging system [2]. CDs with aggregation induced emission (AIE) property are better sensing agents than those suffer from aggregation caused quenching effect (ACQ), as they can have high emission intensity even at elevated concentrations in films. AIE property exhibiting CDs were synthesised from dithiosalycilic acid and melamine and used for developing fluorescent ratiometric tag for sensing volatile basic nitrogen compounds [5,9]. SS bond present in the dithiosalicylic acid precursor imparted AIE and solid state fluorescence properties to the synthesised carbon dots compared to the conventional CDs derived from natural resources.

Packaging materials from natural biopolymers have garnered widespread attention recently, owing to their renewability, rapid biodegradability and nontoxicity [15,16]. k-Carrageenan, a natural sulphated polysaccharide derived from red seaweed Rhodophyceae outstands in food packaging sector attributing to its food compatibility, excellent film forming property, good mechanical property and compostability [17,18]. Carrageenan based intelligent packaging materials are reported for tracking pH change, amine content, temperature variation, oxygen presence, CO2 concentration variation etc. using indicators like anthocyanin, curcumin, carbon dots, bromothymol blue and redox color changing components [10,19–22]. Jiang et al. synthesised intelligent packaging film from carrageenan, pea starch and black raspberry anthocyanin and used for pork freshness monitoring [23]. Sangeetha et al. incorporated pH responsive carbon dots produced from biomass derived lignin and incorporated in carrageenan biopolymer matrix for detecting milk spoilage [10]. Despite their good sensing properties and UV, O2 and CO2 gas barrier characteristics, the poor moisture barrier performance limits their practical applications [10,11,24,25]. High barrier against moisture without sacrificing sensing ability is needed for maintaining the integrity of the packaged food materials as well as extending their shelf life [26]. Coating with bio-based hydrophobic components like zein, stearic acid, vegetable oils, wax etc. are reported to effectively improve water vapour barrier property of natural polymer based packaging materials [27–29]. Zein is a corn derived plant protein with hydrophobic amino acids as major building block and hence having the ability of moisture resistance characteristics [27,30]. Stearic acid, a long alkyl chain containing fatty acid characterized by low surface energy is also having water repellent characteristics and employed in the formulation of water-resistant coatings [15,31].

This work primarily aims at developing a compostable packaging material with synergistic effect of amine sensing and moisture barrier properties. Carbon dots with AIE property has been developed from dithiosalysilic acid and a cheap, readily available and environment friendly raw material urea (Urea-dithiosalicylic acid based carbon dots: UCDs) through solvothemal process and tested for amine sensing capability. Usage of urea as a precursor along with DTSA, instead of reported melamine makes the system more environmental friendly since melamine can easily produce toxic gases like hydrogen cyanide upon thermal decomposition during the synthesis process [5,9,32]. UCDs are further used to make amine sensing intelligent packaging material by incorporating into the carrageenan biopolymer matrix and characterized through various techniques. Further, zein-stearic acid (Z/SA) mixture is applied as a coating to improve the water barrier properties of the system. Carrageenan-UCDs composite film with coating exhibited antioxidant, anti-bacterial and UV blocking properties along with amine sensing ability, which has not been explored so far.

2,2′-Dithiodibenzoic acid (dithiosalycilic acid- DTSA), urea, zein, stearic acid, k-carrageenan and ABTS (2,2′-azino-bis (3-ethylbenzothiazoline-6-sulfonic acid)) were purchased from TCI Chemicals India Pvt. Ltd., Chennai, India. Glacial acetic acid and sulphuric acid were obtained from Spectrum chemicals, Mumbai, India. Magnesium Oxide and glycerol (99.5 %) were procured from Sigma Aldrich Pvt. Ltd., Bangalore, India. Ammonia for sensing study was purchased from Molychem chemicals, Mumbai, India and boric acid from Merck, Mumbai, India. Bacterial strain E. coli and S. aureus with accession numbers MTCC 1610, MTCC 96, were acquired from CSIR-Institute of Microbial Technology, Chandigarh, India. The National Centre for Cell Science (NCCS, 36 Pune, India) provided mouse fibroblast cells (L929). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco's modified Eagle 40 medium (DMEM), buffers and MTT reagent were procured from HiMedia, Mumbai, India. Luria−Bertani (LB) broth and agar powder were purchased from SRL, Mumbai, India. SYTO Green Fluorescent Nucleic Acid Stain (3.34 mM) and propidium iodide (PI) (20 mM) were procured from Invitrogen, Thermo Fisher Scientific, Mumbai, India. All chemicals were used as received without any further purification for all the studies. Deionised water was used for studies with a resistivity value of 18.2 MΩ cm (25 °C).

0.6 g urea and 0.6130 g DTSA were weighed and mixed with 40 mL glacial acetic acid (Fig. 1 ). The mixture was then sonicated for 30 min, transferred to a 250 mL teflon lined hydrothermal autoclave reactor and heated at 200 °C for 10 h in an air oven. After cooling to room temperature, the formed brown coloured solution was purified by passing through 0.22 μm pore sized syringe filter and poured into boiling water. The system was allowed to cool down and the precipitated urea-dithiosalycilic acid based carbon dots (UCDs) were filtered out, washed with large excess of water and dried to obtain an orange color powder (yield:30 %).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of composite films with coating
ce:section.ce:para: In order to make composite film, 1 g of κ-carrageenan was first added to 50 mL of water and stirred at 60 °C, 200 rpm for 30 min. 0.3 g of glycerol was then added to the mixture and stirred again for 30 min in the same reaction condition. UCDs were weighed (2,3 &4 % of carrageenan) and dispersed in ethanol (concentration ∼0.01 g/0.5 mL) via sonication for 5 min and added to the prepared carrageenan solution. The mixture was stirred at 300 rpm for 1 h at 60 °C and then subjected to sonication and vacuum treatment to remove trapped air bubbles in the film forming solution. The whole 50 mL solution was then poured into a poly propylene petri dish (13 cm diameter) and dried in an oven at 50 °C for 10 h. After formation, films were transferred to a desiccator conditioned at 75 % RH (at RT) for 48 h. Carrageenan film is named as CAR and composite films are designated as CARXUCD hereafter, where X denotes UCD's percentages in films.

For preparing the coating solution, stearic acid was first dissolved in ethanol at 40 °C. Then water was added to convert the system into 90 % water-10 % ethanol mixture. After proper mixing and dissolution, zein was added to the system and stirred well until it forms a clear solution which contains 1.5 % of stearic acid and 0.5 % zein in it. Conditioned films were spray coated on one side with zein-SA solution (Z/SA) in three layers, air dried and used for further analysis (Fig. 2 ). The coated samples are designated as CARRXCD-C where X denote the percentage of UCDs in the films. A detailed table summarizing the composition of film forming solutions is given as Table S1 and series of processes are shown as Fig. S1 in SI.
Scalability and production cost (Table S2) of the prepared films are discussed in the supporting information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UCDs characterisations and amine sensing property study
ce:section.ce:para: A high-resolution transmission electron microscope (FEI, Tecnai G2, T30 S-TWIN, FEI Co., Hillsboro, Oregon) running at 300 kV of accelerating potential, controlled by Digital Micrograph software was used to analyze the particle size and morphology of the synthesised CDs. Gatan software was used to analyze the obtained TEM images, and ImageJ software was used to determine the size. DLS (Dynamic Light Scattering) analysis was conducted using Anton Paar, Litesizer 500 instrument with an ingenious software to confirm the particle size of CDs. The elemental composition of the synthesised UCDs were examined using a PHI 5000 Versa probe –II Focus X-Ray Photo Electron Microscope (XPS), USA driven by Smart Soft software which uses a monochromatic Al Kα x-ray source (hv = 1486.6 eV) with a focussed beam (200 μm, 15 kV) for analysis. Using Multipak XPS data processing software (PHI Inc.), the obtained spectra were deconvoluted, and the binding energy was calibrated with respect to the C1s peak at 284.8 eV. FTIR analysis of UCDs and the starting materials was carried out using an ATR-FTIR spectrophotometer (Spectrum Two, Perkin Elmer, U.K.) in atmospheric conditions with 32 scans and a resolution of 4 cm−1. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns in the diffraction angle (2θ) ranging from 10° and 70° were obtained using a 3rd Gen. Empyrean, Malvern Panalytical diffractometer, U.K. using Cu-Kα radiation of wavelength, λ = 1.54 Å and Scherrer constant, K = 0.94.

In order to prepare UCD aggregates, 0.02 g UCDs were dispersed in 10 mL ethanol to obtain 2 mg/mL solution. 0.5 mL of this stock solution was mixed with varying amount of ethanol and water to get solutions with water contents ranging from 0 to 90 vol% with the final UCD concentration of 0.2 mg/mL. UV–Vis absorption spectra of UCDs solutions and powder were recorded using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer, UV-2401PC, Shimadzu, Japan controlled by UV Probe 2.52 software. Fluorescence properties were measured using a HORIBA Jobin Yvon Fluorolog 3 modular spectrofluorometer with a 450 W Xenon flash lamp as the exciting source, driven by FluorEscence V3.5 software to show the aggregation induced emission property.

To check the amine sensing property of UCDs aggregates, 4 mL UCDs solution in 90 % water-10 % ethanol mixture (concentration 0.2 mg/mL) was mixed with 1 mL ammonia solution to get varying final ammonia concentrations ranging from 40 to 500 μM. The photoluminescence (PL) analysis was conducted to check the change happening to UCDs aggregates emission in presence of amines and size change was monitored using DLS analysis using the instruments mentioned above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional group analysis and optical properties
ce:section.ce:para:
Functional group analysis of the films was done using an ATR-FTIR spectrophotometer with the parameters mentioned before. Color parameters of the composite films, L (lighteness), a (red-green) and b (blue-yellow) values were measured using Hunter Lab colorimeter (MiniScan EZ 4500L), Reston, Virginia, USA with a white plate as the standard background. Color difference value ΔE, yellowness index (YI) and whiteness index (WI) were calculated using Eqs. (1), (2) and (3) respectively [33]. ΔL, Δa and Δb are the difference between each color value of composite films with the neat carrageenan film [34].

Film samples were cut into 3 cm × 2 cm size and measured UV–Vis light transmittance through the films using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (UV-2401PC, Shimadzu, Japan) in the range of 200–800 nm [35]. Baseline measurement was taken with the holder remaining empty.

Morphology analysis was conducted using Carl Zeiss EVO MA18 FE SEM, Germany at an accelerating voltage of 15 kV, operated using SmartSEM software. Film thickness were measured using a digital screw gauge with a measurement accuracy of 0.001 mm. X-ray diffraction patterns of the coated and uncoated films was obtained using 3rd Gen. Empyrean, Malvern Panalytical diffractometer, U.K. using Cu-Kα radiation between diffraction angles (2θ) of 10° and 70°. In order to study the surface wettability of films, water contact angle (WCA) was measured at room temperature using an automated DSA 30 Drop Shape Analyzer. KRUSS, Germany in sessile drop method.

Thermal stability of the films was analysed by heating the samples (7–8 mg) from 30 to 700 °C under nitrogen atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in a thermogravimetric analyser (SII Nanotechnology Inc., Japan, EXSTAR series TGA/DTG 6200). Instrument uses STARe Excellence Thermal Analysis Software for data acquisition and analysis. Thermal transitions occurring in the films were studied using Differential Scanning Calorimetric (DSC) analysis using Q20, M/s TA Instrument, USA at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in the temperature range of 30 °C to 200 °C in nitrogen atmosphere.

Mechanical properties of the films were tested using a universal testing machine (Instron 3382, UK) according to ASTM standard, ASTM D882. Films were first cut into rectangles having 15 mm width and 90 mm length and measurement was conducted with cross head speed of 5 mm/min and a gauge length of 50 mm.

Volatile amines sensing property of CAR3UCD-C film was tested using ammonia as a representative gas. Film strips of size 1 cm × 1 cm were kept on top of different testing chambers (175 mL capacity) at room temperature (30 °C) and humidity (70 % RH) condition with uncoated side of film facing inside of container. Ammonia solution was added to the chambers and stand for 1 h to get a final ammonia concentration of 0, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 and 35 μM. The concentration of ammonia in the chamber was calculated using Eq. (4) by following Song et al. [36]
(4)

mol L

W S

where C is the concentration of the substance under testing in the chamber (mol/L), ρs is the analysing substance density (g/mL), Vs is the volume of liquid substance (mL), Ws is the assay of analyte solution, M is the molar mass of the sample to be tested (g/mol) and V is the volume of the container. Fluorescent color parameters of films were measured before and after exposure to ammonia gas. For that, photographs were taken using a smartphone after keeping films inside a dark box (during day time) having 365 nm wavelength lamp inside (Spectroline-CM-10A fluorescence analysis cabinet with 365 nm lamp, MODEL ENF-260C/FE). The captured photographs were used to calculate corresponding R,G,B values with the help of Adobe Photoshop CS6 software and limit of detection (LOD) of ammonia gas was determined from the graph of R/B value plotted against ammonia concentration by using the Eq. (5)
(5)
LOD =
3 σ
N
where σ is the standard deviation of blank measurement and N is the slope of the calibration curve plotted by taking ammonia concentration on X axis and R/B value on Y axis.

The CAR3UCD-C film was exposed to other volatile solvents in addition to ammonia gas, in order to confirm the sensitivity of the film to ammonia gas alone. Film strips of size 1 cm × 1 cm were placed on top of testing chambers (175 mL capacity) at ambient conditions, with uncoated side of the films facing inside of the container. 3 mL of volatile solvents (ethanol, methanol, ethyl acetate, water, acetone, acetic acid (glacial), dichloromethane) and 0.3 ml 25 % ammonia solution were added to various chambers separately and left to stand for 15 min. Images were taken and R,G,B values were calculated same as before [9,36]. Three set of measurements were taken for each solvent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Moisture barrier property and moisture content
ce:section.ce:para:
Water vapour transmission rate (WVTR) was determined by taking three specimens for each sample by following ASTM E96, using the desiccant method. Mason jars filled with dried desiccant were covered with lids containing film samples sealed on it. Weight change of the jar was measured over time and it was used to measure the WVTR value according to the standard. Oven drying method was used to find moisture content present in the prepared films. Films were cut into 2 cm × 2 cm pieces, weighed (Wo (g)) and kept in an air oven at 105 °C for 24 h and the sample was weighed again (W1 (g)). Moisture content was calculated using Eq. (6) and is expressed as a percentage of initial film weight [10,37].
(6)
Moisture content
%
=
W o
−
W 1
W o
∗ 100

ABTS radical cation decolorisation assay was conducted by adapting the method we have published earlier with some modifications [10,38]. ABTS radicals were formed via the reaction of 2 mL, 7 mM aqueous ABTS solution and 35.2 μM potassium persulfate solution. The radicals containing solution was diluted to an initial absorbance value of 0.7 ± 0.05 at 734 nm after incubating for 16 h in dark condition. Then the solution was treated with ethanolic solution of UCDs and 0.1 g of synthesised films separately, and the absorbance was measured after incubation in a dark environment for 30 min. Radical scavenging activity against ABTS was calculated using Eq. (7).
(7)
% ABTS scavenging activity =
A 0
−
A 1
A 0
× 100
where A0 is the absorbance of pure ABTS solution and A1 is the absorbance with the ABTS solution containing sample to be analysed, at 734 nm.

The antibacterial efficacy of the samples was monitored using the agar well diffusion method [39]. For this investigation, both Gram-positive S. aureus (MTCC 96) and Gram-negative E. coli (MTCC 1610) bacterial strains were employed. Each bacterial isolate was suspended in Luria Bertani broth (LB) and diluted to colony forming units (CFU) per milliliter. The spread plate methods were carried out in LB agar plate followed by adjustment of pH value to 8.0. The film samples (4 mm2) were then placed on the agar plate. The experiment was carried out in triplicate and incubated at 37 °C. Afterwards the zone of inhibition was measured accordingly. The bacterial cell viability was monitored using bacterial live/dead staining assay using solution of SYTO™ Green Fluorescent Nucleic Acid Stain and propidium iodide (PI). 50 μL of SYTO 9 working solution, 50 μL of PI working solution, and/or 0.85 % saline (for a total amount of 100 μL) were added to 0.9 ml of culture in an amber tube to create stained samples to achieve 1.67 and 20 μM final concentrations, respectively. SYTO 9 and PI were employed to stain each living and dead cell sample under Invitrogen EVOSTM fluorescence imaging system.

The CV assay study evaluated the antimicrobial potency of sample towards E. coli and S. aureus by inoculating them with 96-well plates containing samples. The cultures were incubated for 48 h at 37 °C, and the media was aspirated and washed. The biofilm was fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde and stained with 0.1 % crystal violet. The plates were dried and the absorbance was read at 595 nm. To characterize the grown biofilm, specimens were washed with PBS, fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde and 1 % osmium tetroxide (OsO4) and dehydrated with graded ethanol. The specimens processed for SEM analysis (model: ZEISS EVO 18) by successive dehydration with graded alcohol and sputter coated with gold‑palladium coating under vacuum evaporator. The negative control in this study was maintained as the absolute film formation with only cultures and positive control without any bio film production (no culture).

In order to assess the biocompatibility of films, the MTT assay was performed [40]. Fibroblast cell line (L929) was cultivated at 37 °C in 5 % CO2 in DMEM medium with 10 % FBS and 1 % penicillin-streptomycin. L929 cells were seeded into 96-well plates with a 5 × 104 number, and the cells were subsequently incubated for 24 h with the samples. MTT dye (5 mg/mL) was added to each well, followed by PBS wash and incubation at 37 °C. The absorbance at 570 nm was then measured using Thermo Fisher Scientific Varioskan LUX Multimode Microplate Reader after the addition of 200 μL of DMSO to each well and gentle agitation to dissolve the formazan crystals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real-time sensing of prawn freshness monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: 50 g prawns were kept in a plastic container (175 mL), covered with a lid with the developed film as a small window (1 cm2) on it and kept in a refrigerator at 4 °C (three sets were kept parallelly). Fluorescent color change of the attached film was observed under UV light (365 nm) inside a dark box as mentioned before and photograph was taken on every 48 h for 8 days. Similar test was conducted at room temperature conditions (30 °C) as well and the film emission color change monitored after a day. Same amount of prawns was kept in parallel under the same conditions and it was used for finding Total Volatile Basic Nitrogen (TVBN) value using Kjeldahl nitrogen analyser by following Y. Xu et al. with some modifications [5]. The prawns sample (10 g) was homogenised with 100 mL water and filtered to collect the liquid part. 10 mL of this filtrate was treated with 2 mL, 1 wt% MgO solution in water, introduced into kjeldahl nitrogen analyser and distilled for 5 min. The distillate was absorbed by 20 g/L H3BO3 and titrated using 0.02 N H2SO4 to quantify TVBN content.

Compostability of the samples was analysed using soil burial test [28]. Each sample was cut into 2.5 cm × 2.5 cm size, dried at 60 °C until weight become constant and buried in plastic containers filled with soil collected from outside. Soil was sprayed with water in regular intervals of time to ensure same moisture condition in all samples. Films were taken out on day 9, 18 and 27 respectively and the soil particles were removed thoroughly by washing. Weight of these collected samples were measured after drying in oven at 60 °C until its weight reach a constant value. The weight loss percentage of sample was measured using Eq. (8).
(8)
Weight loss % =
W i
−
W f
W i
× 100
where Wi (g) is the initial weight of the sample and Wf (g) is the final dry weight of the sample after degradation in soil.

All experiments have been carried out in triplicate. Mean was taken as the final result and reported with standard deviation. Data were analysed by one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) followed by Tukey test in origin (Origin Pro 2016 64 Bit) software. A p value < 0.05 is considered as statistically significant.

UCDs are synthesised from dithiosalycilic acid and urea through a one pot solvothermal process using environment friendly low cost chemical, acetic acid as solvent. Acetic acid act as a catalyst for carbonisation reaction, in addition to its role as a reaction solvent [41]. The obtained HR TEM images of UCDs (Fig. 3(a)) reveal that the particles are having spherical shape with the diameter of ∼10 nm. These particles have high tendency of aggregation and tend to agglomerate on TEM grid as seen in the electron micrograph. The DLS analysis (Fig. 3(b)) substantiates the size analysis observation from TEM, confirming UCDs with average hydrodynamic radius of ∼10 nm in the ethanol medium.

The elemental composition and functional group analysis of UCDs are explored using XPS and FTIR analysis techniques. The XPS survey spectrum in Fig. 3(c) shows four different peaks at 532.04 eV, 399.24 eV, 284.04 eV, and 164.04 eV suggesting that the system contain O, N, C, and S elements, and the atomic ratios were calculated as 13.8 %, 3 %, 76.6 %, and 6.6 % respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of the C1s band (Fig. 3(d)) can be deconvoluted into four different peaks at 288.53 eV, 285.68 eV, 285.06 eV, and 284.99 eV, attributing to CN/CO, CO, CN and CC/CC bonds, respectively [42]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 3(e)) contains three different peaks at 400.19 eV, 399.26 eV and 398.77 eV corresponding to pyrrolic, amide and pyridinic nitrogen atoms existing as a part of the carbon core and surface. The spectrum of O2s (Fig. 3(f)) exhibits two peaks, one at 533.37 eV for COC/COH and the other at 531.85 eV for CO groups. Similarly, high resolution S2p spectrum (Fig. 3(g)) contains two peaks at 164.65 eV and 163.50 eV originating from the SS and SC bonds [43]. The XPS spectrum clearly indicates the successful doping of nitrogen atoms during carbonisation process and reveals the functionalities present in UCDs.

As shown in FTIR spectra (Fig. 4(a)), hydrophilic functionalities such as amino group (3427 cm−1) from urea and hydroxyl groups (3067 cm−1) from DTSA got highly diminished after carbon dot formation indicating the reduced hydrophilicity of CDs formed during the carbonisation process. UCDs contain peaks corresponding to CS (694 cm−1), CO (1147 cm−1), CC (1459 cm−1), CO of acid and amide (1674 cm−1, 1613 cm−1) and SH (2657 cm−1) bonds, which are present in its structural backbone [41]. Additionally, a new peak appeared in the UCDs at 1342 cm−1 attributed to the amide III band arising from the amide formation during the solvothermal process. Peak noticed at 555 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of SS bonds present in the system, which plays a crucial role in the AIE proprty [9]. Mentioned FTIR peaks are tabulated as Table S3 in SI.

The XRD patterns in Fig. 4(b) showed intense and sharp peaks due to the ordered structure of UCDs in the powdered state. The most prominent peak at 26° corresponds to the (002) plane of graphite which is reported in the previous studies [43]. XRD pattern of UCDs match well with that of DTSA, indicating the formation of CDs through a series of reactions including polymerisation and carbonisation of DTSA during the solvothermal reaction [44]. UCDs are slightly amorphous compared to DTSA as seen in the XRD pattern. This amorphous nature is a result of disruption of ordered structure of DTSA during carbonisation reaction caused by the interaction with urea and incorporation of nitrogen atoms in the structure, as discussed in FTIR and XPS analyses [45].

The formed UCDs are soluble in ethanol and it appear as a transparent yellowish liquid with blue emission under 365 nm light as shown in Fig. 4(c). Once water content (volume ratio) is increased in the medium, solution gradually turns into a turbid liquid due to the agglomeration of hydrophobic UCDs and the fluorescence color changes to orange. UCDs powder exhibit orange-brown color under visible light and bright orange fluorescent color under 365 nm light (Fig. 1).

The UV–Vis absorption and PL emission properties of UCDs were measured as a function of varying ethanol-water content and the results are depicted in Fig. 4(d) and (e). The absorption peak of UCDs at 309 nm can be attributed to the π-π* transitions of CC bonds in the aromatic structure. Two peaks observed at 450 nm (abs-1) and 550 nm (abs-2) correspond to the n-π* transitions of surface functional groups, COC/COH, SC and CO,SS, respectively [43]. As the water content in the medium increased to 90 vol%, peak absorbance gradually decreased due to the aggregation of hydrophobic UCDs in the system. Once nanoparticles aggregate, their overall surface area diminishes, which restricts the number of sites available for light interaction resulting in reduced UV absorption as showcased. Fluorescence emission spectra of UCDs underscores the aggregation induced emission color change property. UCDs get dissolved in ethanol completely and give a blue emission centered at ∼400 nm with a shoulder peak at ∼468 nm. As the volume fraction of non-solvent water increases, blue light from the dissolved state and orange light from the aggregated state started to compete each other. Once the water volume ratio reaches to 80 %, the system becomes cloudy, and the orange emission starts to dominate. The intensity of the peak at 400 nm decreases, while two new peaks emerge at 495 nm (em-1) and 593 nm (em-2). At 90 % v/v of water, the peak at 413 nm completely diminished, while the other two peaks become more pronounced, resembling the solid-state UCD emission spectra (Fig. 4(f)). Excitation spectrum recorded at em-2 overlapped with the abs-2 and the excitation spectrum recorded at em-1 overlapped with the abs-1, as shown in Fig. 4(f). This indicates that the two absorption bands correspond to two different luminescence centres in UCDs. Moreover, the peak position of em-1 overlaps with the excitation band recorded at em-2, which indicates that the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) process occurs in the system [43]. In 80 % water medium, UCDs form loose aggregates, leading to higher emission intensity for em-1 than em-2. At 90 % water, tighter aggregates effectively reduce the distance between UCDs, further increasing em-2 intensity. When converted to solid powder, em-2 intensity further increases due to closer interparticle distances, enhancing FRET efficiency.

The reason for the change in emission color of UCDs upon aggregation is as follows. In solution form, emissions from the graphitised core of UCDs are dominant, while those from the surface states are quenched due to the non-radiative transition of absorbed energy owing to intramolecular motions. When UCDs assemble due to hydrophobicity in the higher water-containing medium, the carbon core gets subjected to ACQ due to π-π stacking interactions and it turns off short wavelength emission. On the other hand, restricted intramolecular rotation (RIR) around disulphide bonds on the surface helps to exhibit long wavelength fluorescent emission contributing to the aggregation-induced emission property as shown in Fig. 5 [41]. It can be noticed that the PL emission spectra recorded at different excitation wavelengths for UCDs in ethanol solution (Fig. S2(a) in SI) show a red shift with an increase in excitation wavelength similar to traditional carbon dots [46,47]. While in aggregated (Fig. S2(b) in SI) and powder form (Fig. 4(g)), emission is independent of excitation wavelength comparable to inorganic traditional fluorophores [41]. This observation further substantiates the inference that UCD emissions come from carbon core in solution state and from surface states in aggregated form.

Sensing performance of UCDs aggregates in a 90 % water-10 % ethanol solvent system towards ammonia is shown in the Fig. 6 . Upon increasing the concentration of ammonia in the media from 0 to 500 μM, fluorescence emission peak intensity at 495 nm and 593 nm got diminished and the one at 413 nm got enhanced gradually (Fig. 6(a)). The PL intensity ratio, I553/I413 showed a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.9893) with the concentration of ammonia in the media with an LOD of 18.6 μM (Fig. 6(b)). The change in emission color is related to the change in functionalities on the surface of UCDs after interaction with ammonia molecules as well as the disintegration of UCD aggregates. FTIR analysis of UCDs after exposure with ammonia gas confirms the reaction of ammonia molecules with functionalities present on the carbon dots surface. A new band at 3177 cm−1 attributed to the NH stretching vibration is noticed after the interaction with ammonia. Additionally, the carbonyl peak at 1677 cm−1 got significantly diminished, suggesting the imine bond formation of the carbonyl group with NH₃ molecules (Fig. S3 in SI). Fluorescence spectra of UCDs powder before and after exposure indicates that em-2 intensity (arising from CO containing surface groups) is readily decreasing with ammonia exposure due to the decrease in concentration of emission sites containing the CO group (Fig. S4 in SI). It can be concluded that the presence of NH groups altered the surface functionality of UCDs, transforming their nature from hydrophobic to hydrophilic. This change results in the de-agglomeration of UCDs in aqueous media and leads to their dissolution. DLS analysis (Fig. S5(a) in SI) shows that the diameter of the UCD aggregates decreased gradually with the increase in ammonia concentration in the medium. Similarly, the increased negative value of zeta potential after interaction with amine indicates that the system became electrically stabilised after interaction with ammonia molecules (Fig. S5(b) in SI). The change in functionality and dissolution process passivated the AIE property of UCDs and resulted in the emission color change from orange to blue as shown in Fig. 6(c).

The FTIR spectra of the synthesised films are analysed to identify their surface functional groups (Fig. 7(a)). Carrageenan film (CAR) exhibited peaks at 3355 cm−1 (OH stretching), 2916 cm−1 (CH stretching), 1647 cm−1 (bound water), 1244 cm−1 (sulfate group), 1032 cm−1 (glycosidic bonds), 919 cm−1 (CO stretching of 3,6-anhydrogalactose) and 842 cm−1 (CO stretching of galactose-4-sulfate) [10,48]. After the addition of UCDs, new peaks appeared within 1450–1600 cm−1 owing to the presence of aromatic rings from the carbon dot's structure and the peak intensity increased in a concentration dependent manner. After coating with zein-stearic acid mixture, film surface got grafted with hydrophobic functional moieties which mask the inherent functional groups in carrageenan and UCDs in all cases. Coated sample surface contains one major broad peak at 3298 cm−1 arising from the NH and OH stretching bands arising from the functionalities of coating mixture [49]. The mentioned peak is more broad compared to the peak present in pure zein (Fig. S6 in SI) due to the hydrogen bonding interaction between zein, stearic acid and carrageenan which reveals a good adhesion at the interface. CH2 group asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations from stearic acid results in two peaks at 2916 cm−1 and 2848 cm−1 respectively [50]. Two distinct carbonyl groups, one from stearic acid carboxyl moiety and the other from zein amide overlap and appear in between 1699 cm−1 and 1651 cm−1. Amide II band, appearing at 1533 cm−1 in pure zein shifted to 1544 cm−1 in the coating due to the NH and CN bonds interaction with functional groups present in stearic acid and those on the surface of composite films. FTIR spectra analysis confirms the interaction between components in coating as well as with the film surface. Discussed FTIR peaks are tabulated in Table S4 in SI.

The visual appearance of pure carrageenan and UCDs incorporated composite films (with and without Z/SA coating) is shown in Fig. 7(b). Neat carrageenan film (CAR) is found to be colorless with good readability underneath. While incorporation of UCDs imparted an orange color to the composite film, and the color intensity increased with the increase in carbon dots content. This resulted in a significant (p ≤ 0.05) color difference (ΔE) compared to CAR film with a reduction in the whiteness index and an increase in the yellowness index, as detailed in the Table 1 . The coating did not notably affect the film's appearance or color properties; the color parameters remained almost unchanged after the surface coating with p > 0.05 compared to the uncoated counterparts. The readability of the films is still maintained despite the addition of carbon dots and even after surface coating. Furthermore, the intensity of the orange fluorescence emission in the films increased with increase in concentration of UCDs (Fig. S7(a) in SI). Trend of increase in emission intensity with increase in UCDs concentration was maintained even after coating as showcased in Fig. 7(b) and Fig. S7(c). Normalised emission intensity spectra of CAR2UCD, CAR3UCD and CAR4UCD films (Fig. S7(b) in SI) further support the FRET concept as discussed in earlier sessions. As the concentration of UCDs in the films increased, the proximity among the particles facilitated the FRET process, leading to a reduction in the emission intensity of em-1 (495 nm) and an enhancement in the emission intensity of em-2 (593 nm).

To evaluate the transparency in the visible light region, the optimum transmittance of the films is noticed at a wavelength of 660 nm (Fig. 7(c)) [51]. Carrageenan film displayed the highest transparency value of 91.95 %, wherein the incorporation of UCDs reduced transparency values to 84.35 %, 83.45 %, and 80.31 % for the CAR2UCD, CAR3UCD, and CAR4UCD films, respectively. In addition, corresponding coated films (CAR2UCD-C, CAR3UCD-C, and CAR4UCD-C) exhibited transparency values of 82.05 %, 81.53 %, and 79.19 %, which are quite similar to those of their uncoated counterparts.

UV light is highly detrimental to maintaining the nutritional value and organoleptic qualities (such as odour, flavour, and color) of packaged food materials since it can induce photocatalytic oxidation of fats, natural pigments, and other compounds in them, leading to food spoilage [38,52]. Therefore, the UV light transmittance properties of packaging films have been studied, and the results are illustrated in Fig. 7(c). The neat carrageenan film (CAR) exhibited a UV light transmittance of 71.02 % at 280 nm. In contrast, composite films containing 2 %, 3 %, and 4 % of UCDs demonstrated a significantly reduced UV light transmittance of 20.07 %, 12.96 %, and 6.49 %, respectively. Carbon dots exhibit high UV light-blocking property owing to their capability to absorb UV radiation through n-π* and π-π* electronic transitions of the functional groups present in them [53]. Additionally, the incorporation of Z/SA coating further improved the UV-blocking capabilities of these composite films. UV light transmittance values reduced by 90.38 %, 92.43 %, and 97.19 % compared to CAR film for CAR2UCD-C, CAR3UCD-C and CAR4UCD-C samples respectively. This can be attributed to the presence of UV light absorbing aromatic amino acids in zein protein, one of the components in the applied coating [54]. Thus, UCDs incorporation and coating makes the composite film a suitable packaging material in the food industry.

The surface and crossectional morphology of films are investigated through SEM micrographs as presented in Fig. 8(a) and (b). Pure carageenan film shows smoothness in the surface as well as cross-sectional areas without any cracks or defects, indicating its good film formation capability. UCDs incorporation resulted in increased surface roughness of films and the lumps size increased with increase in UCDs concentration. While, cross-sectional image shows good compatibility between carrageenan biopolymer and UCDs without having voids, microcracks or significant micro phase separation (Fig. 8(a)). The considerable roughness and aggregate formation on the surface may be attributed to the changes induced by the solvent evaporation process. Carrageenan is hydrophilic and incorporated carbon dots are relatively hydrophobic in nature. When the solvent evaporation happens on the surface, hydrophobic components may have the tendency to aggregate and form microscale roughness as observed in the images (Fig. 8(a)). The Z/SA coating imaprted changes in surface morphology of the nanocomposite film (Fig. 8(b)). The SEM images confirms the successful surface coverage of the films by hydrophobic coating material which resulted in an increase in surface roughness. Stearic acid flakes are bound together by zein protein, which helps to hold hydrophobic stearic acid on top of the hydrophilic carrageenan biopolymer film. The coating is having good adherence on film surface without any gap between the two as observed in the cross-sectional image of the coated film, confirming the strong bonding of Z/SA layer with carrageenan matrix. Cross-sectional surface images of the CAR3UCD-C film show a coating thickness of approximately 20 μm, which aligns with the thickness value measured physically (Fig. S8 in SI).

The XRD pattern of the composite films closely resemble that of pure carrageenan, featuring a broad peak centered at an angle (2θ) of 20.19° (Fig. 8(c)). Compared to pure carrageenan, composite films didn't show any new diffraction peak. The result indicates that crystallinity of carrageenan was not affected by UCDs probably due to the uniform distribution and low concentration of these nanoparticles in the polymer matrix [51,55]. A similar result was reported when glucose derived CDs were incorporated in cellulose nano fiber film and chitosan based carbon dots in carboxy methyl cellulose film [55,56]. Coated film XRD analysis further confirms the successful coating of the film surface with the Z/SA mixture. After surface coating, the XRD peaks became sharper as shown in Fig. 8(d), due to the presence of stearic acid flakes on the surface, which is observed in the SEM analysis. The surface is uniformly covered with a mixture of zein and stearic acid, effectively masking the broad XRD pattern of the carrageenan film beneath, aligning with the results observed in FTIR and SEM analysis. XRD pattern of pure zein and stearic acid given in Fig. 8(e) confirms that the sharp peaks in diffraction pattern of coated films are arising from the crystallinity of stearic acid.

The surface wettability of films has been assessed by measuring their static contact angles (CAs) and the values are depicted in Fig. 9(a). Pure carrageenan film exhibited a CA value of 69.3°. The droplet placed on the sample surface for measurement got absorbed almost completely and the sample became swollen within 5 min leading to a zero contact angle value. All the samples, including CAR2UCD, CAR3UCD, and CAR4UCD films exhibited similar contact angle value and swelled in a manner similar to that of the pure carrageenan matrix without any significant change (p > 0.05). Coating with the hydrophobic components, Z/SA significantly (p ≤ 0.05) raised the average value of CAs above 90° for all the nano composite films. The coated films (CAR2UCD-C, CAR3UCD-C, and CAR4UCD-C) exhibited minimal water absorption, resulting in a sustained contact angle value of over 79°, demonstrating their ability to resist water absorption. [28]. This water absorption difference is more clearly shown in Fig. 9(b) and (c), where the uncoated sample swelled significantly, while the coated sample displayed no visible signs of absorption. This observed change in water absorption can be attributed to the hydrophobicity of saturated long alkyl chain of stearic acid as well as the relatively hydrophobic nature (75 % hydrophobic moieties) of the binding agent, zein protein [57–59]. Here, the Z/SA coating makes a continuous layer on the hydrophilic polymer carrageenan, which impart water resistant property to the composite film.

Thermal stability characteristics of the developed films were studied and the results are shown in Fig. 9(d) and Fig. S9 in SI. It is observed that all films undergo multiple stages of degradation corresponding to various components and functional groups present in the system. Weight loss occurring below 140 °C in all cases can be attributed to the removal of moisture adsorbed to the films via hydrogen bonding interactions. The next stage of degradation, in the range of 150–220 °C is resulting from degradation of glycerol, added to the films as a plasticizer. A drastic degradation occurs there after corresponds to the elimination of sulphate groups present on the carrageenan polymer backbone. Degradation of stearic acid in the coated samples also took place in the same temperature range and overlapped with sulphate group degradation [60]. For the coated films, the next stage of degradation occurs between 240 and 275 °C which corresponds to the thermal degradation of zein biopolymer in the coating [61]. The degradation profile is similar for both the coated and uncoated films thereafter. Weight loss occurring after 275 °C originates from the carbohydrate backbone degradation of the carrageenan biopolymer. The TGA profiles of all films are very similar, indicating that the addition of UCDs and surface coatings does not impact the thermal stability of the base polymer, carrageenan. All of these materials exhibit thermal stability till 200 °C, which is sufficient for food packaging applications.

The DSC thermographs of developed films (Fig. 9(e)) showed only a minute increase in glass transition temperature (41.9 °C to 43.76 °C) with increase in carbon dots content in the system [62]. A broad endothermic peak is noticed for all composite films between 50 °C–135 °C, attributed to the presence of adsorbed water in the system and the peak temperature for this transition (Tp), which is 78 °C for neat carrageenan got shifted to a high temperature (∼89 °C) value after UCDs incorporation [63]. This change can be ascribed to the interaction of functional groups present on the surface of UCDs with those present in the carrageenan polymer backbone which control the mobility of polymer chains upon heating process and subsequent release of water from the system [64,65]. In addition to this, one new peak is appearing in the range of 62–72 °C in the case of coated films, arising from the melting of stearic acid flakes present on the coated surface as observed in the morphology analysis. Coating reduced the glass transition temperature to a lower value compared to the uncoated films, mostly due to the interaction of ethanol solvent with the carrageenan polymer chains during the coating process.

Tensile properties play an important role in film's food packaging applicability. Neat carrageenan film displayed an average tensile strength (TS) value of 70.80 MPa with an elongation at break (EAB) of 10.87 % as shown in Fig. 10(a). Corresponding values of the UCDs added composite films remained unchanged (p > 0.05), which indicates that the addition of fillers had no impact on the tensile characteristics of the base polymer. This may be attributed to the uniform distribution of carbon dots in the carrageenan matrix which doesn't alter the polymer chain interactions significantly [35]. A similar result was reported by Khan et al. where the incorporation of ZnO-doped CDs in the carrageean matrix resulted in no change in the tensile strength of the developed packaging film [22]. Glucose derived carbon dots when incorporated in cellulose nano fiber also didn't change the tensile properties of the native film [56]. Once the coating was applied to the film, the TS value reduced and EAB improved slightly as shown in Fig. 10(a). This may be due to the plasticization of the carrageenan matrix upon exposure to ethanol solvent used in the coating process. Upon exposure with low concentrations of ethanol, carrageenan may lose the hydrogen bonding interactions between polymer chains, resulting in decreased tensile strength and the result can be correlated with the reduction in Tg value observed in DSC analysis [66]. A similar observation was found when Bu et al. coated Nano-ZnO/(3-Aminopropyl) Triethoxysilane/Stearic Acid in ethanol on gelatin nanocomposite film [15]. However, the tensile strength of the coated composite films (∼50 MPa) is comparable to the commodity plastics used in the packaging like PP (27–42 MPa), linear LDPE (37 MPa), PET(8–72 MPa) etc. [15,67], indicating the potential of films for food packaging application.

Fish spoilage process can produce variety of volatile amines in its environment and ammonia is one of the representative among them. Hence, ammonia sensing study was conducted using CAR3UCD-C film and the results are shown in Fig. 10(b), (c), (d) and (e). Preliminary studies showed that 3 % is the maximum amount of UCDs that can be incorporated in films to have good amine responsiveness, hence it is used for detailed ammonia sensing studies. It is clear from the photographs under UV light (365 nm) that the fluorescent color of the film changed gradually from orange to blue with the increase in ammonia concentration in the sensing system (Fig. 10(b)). The results shows that UCDs maintain its potential to sense ammonia even when it is incorporated in polymer matrix. The fluorescent color changes of the films are described in terms of R,G,B values measured using Adobe Photoshop CS6. A good linear correlation was observed between the R/B value and the concentration of ammonia in the range of 0–35 μM, and the graph is shown in Fig. 10(c). The corresponding calibration curve is expressed as Eq. (9),
(9)
y = 1.5229 − 0.0311 x
R 2
= 0.9812
where x is the concentration of ammonia (μM) and y is the R/B value. Limit of detection was calculated to be 0.6 μM. A comparison of amine detection limit (LOD) of film developed in this work with other reported systems is given in Table 2 . The comparison shows that the composite film is having good potential to be used as an ammonia sensing agent for intelligent packaging applications. It is interesting to note that the films after exposure with ammonia did not exhibit reverse fluorescent color change by keeping in ambient atmosphere for 7 days (Fig. S10 in SI). This property attributes more credibility to the intelligent packaging tag in a realtime scenario since no fraudulent action is possible once the tag is contacted with amines from spoiled meat.

Selectivity of the CAR3UCD-C film was investigated using various volatile compounds (ethanol, acetone, methanol, ethyl acetate, water, dichloromethane, acetic acid and ammonia) which can be produced during meat spoilage. As shown in Fig. 10(d) and (e), all gases other than ammonia produced no significant visible change (p > 0.05) in fluorescence color of the composite film. The fluorescence response was quantified by the R/B value, which indicate that R/B value remained above 1.3 for ethanol, acetone, methanol, ethyl acetate, water, dichloromethane and acetic acid after exposure for 15 min. In case of ammonia, blue color became dominant, which resulted in a significant reduction (p ≤ 0.05) of R/B value, <0.5. The results indicate that CAR3UCD-C film is having good selectivity towards volatile amines. This sensitivity and selectivity towards amine can be explained based on the change in FTIR spectra of UCDs (Fig. S2 in SI) as discussed earlier in session 3.1. The intensity of the carbonyl peak significantly decreased after interacting with ammonia, which is attributed to the formation of imine bonds. Carbonyl groups are the primary contributors to the emission peak around 600 nm (em-2), resulting in the sample's orange-coloured emission. As the exposure time increases, the intensity of this particular peak in film emission spectra decreases rapidly, which aligns with the amine‑carbonyl reaction predicted from the FTIR results (Fig. S11). Other solvents are not able to chemically react with the functionalities responsible for orange emission and hence are not able to influence the emission color of CAR3UCD-C films. A schematic representation of composite film color change with exposure to ammonia is shown in Fig. 11 .

Shelf life of most of the food materials depends on the water vapour permeability of the packaging material, as moisture can increase the rate of lipid oxidation, microbial growth, and the browning reaction of food components [11]. Water vapour transmission rate (WVTR) of film samples are determined in this regard to understand the barrier performance of films towards water vapour (Fig. 12(a)). Carrageenan is a hydrophilic natural biopolymer which exhibit high value of WVTR (236 g/(m2.day)) due to the high chance of solubility and diffusivity of water molecules through the matrix. It is observed that UCDs do not enhance the water barrier properties of the hydrophilic carrageenan matrix significantly (p > 0.05). Consequently, a moisture-resistant hydrophobic coating is adopted, leading to a significant reduction (p ≤ 0.05) in WVTR by ∼84 % (from 236 g/(m2.day) for CAR to 36 g/(m2.day) for CAR3UCD-C). This drastic change can be attibuted to the hydrophobic property of long alkyl chain (C18) containing stearic acid which is bound together by zein protein on the composite film surface [27]. A similar trend was observed in the stearic acid coated PVA film, where 3-aminopropyl trimethoxysilane (APTMS) served as a bridge between the hydrophobic stearic acid and hydrophilic PVA matrix [58]. In a similar way, microcrystalline cellulose ester/stearic acid coating reduced the WVTR of baggase cellulose paper sheets, when it was spray coated on the surface [71]. In another study, improvement in hydrophobicity of cellulose nanofibrils film was achieved by coating with stearic acid and stearic acid modified calcium carbonate, which indicate the efficiency of stearic acid in tuning WVTR of films [72]. Here, zein protein helps to address the incompatibility between carragenan and stearic acid and help to achieve good hydrophobic material coverage on the hydrophilic surface of carrageenan (It is to be noted that the coating didn't have any effect on the moisture content of films (Fig. S12 in SI) since coating is applied only on one side and the other part is exposed to the air during test). WVTR results indicate that coated films are having good moisture barrier property in addition to amine responsiveness, which will help to enhance the shelf life of packaged food materials.

Oxidation of packaged food materials can reduce its shelf life and decrease the expected storage time and consumption. Anti-oxidant packaging materials can reduce such oxidative degradation of food items and protect them from early spoilage [29]. Carbon dots are reported to have antioxidant activity and are utilised in packaging to impart active properties. Therefore, the antioxidant activity of UCDs and the films were determined by ABTS assay and the results are depicted in Fig. 12(b) and (c). UCDs exhibited high anti-oxidant activity against ABTS•+ radicals and the activity increased significantly (p ≤ 0.05) with upsurge in concentration. The starting materials for UCDs, urea and DTSA both showed very less anti-oxidant capacity compared to UCDs as shown in the bar diagram given in Fig. S13 in SI. This indicates that carbonisation, formation of new functional groups and doping occurred during the solvothermal reaction which impart active properies to the formed UCDs. It is reported that doped electron donating sites and sp2 conjugated carbon core in CDs can result in destruction of free radicals via formation of adducts which cause spin delocalisation across the carbon core [73]. Neat carrageenan film showed poor anti oxidxant activity with a scavenging power of 23.30 %. Composite film with 3 % carbon dots exhibited a significantly high (p ≤ 0.05) radical scavenging activity (83.96 %) owing to the antioxidant nature of UCDs. Antioxidant potential reduced slightly (activity-70.09 %) after coating with zein-stearic acid mixture on one side of the film because of the decreased interaction of UCDs with aqueous ABTS radicals, caused by the hydrophobic nature of the coating layer. The results indicate that the synthesised films are having good radical scavenging activity in addition to the intelligent sensing property which make it a potential packaging material.

Zone of inhibition study against gram negative (E. coli) and gram-positive (S. aureus) bacteria was performed to investigate the antimicrobial potency of the samples. Pure carrageenan film (CAR) did not show any antimicrobial activity, resulting in no zone of inhibition. Whereas in case of composite films, prominent zone of inhibition was noticed with antimicrobial activity increasing with increase in UCDs concentration (Fig. 13(a)) mostly due to the bacterial cell membrane disruption and leakage through oxidative stress induction by UCDs which ultimately leads to DNA damage [74]. Antimicrobial efficacy was further validated by SEM micrographs wherein significant alteration of cell morphology protracted upon bacteria by coated and uncoated composite film samples (Fig. 13(b)). Bacteria treated with UCDs incorporated films showed ruptured cellular morphology with altered cell membrane integrity due to the total malfunction in bacterial cell membrane caused by surface adsorption of CD molecules. Specifically, after incubating with CAR3UCD-C film, no structural integrity was found, cells are clumping and no prominent architecture was visible in bacterial culture. This indicates that Z/SA coating, which is applied as a hydrophobic layer to improve moisture barrier properties additionally enhances the antibacterial activity of films. These components leads to the denaturation of microbial enzymes and involve in lipid bilayer disruption of bacterial cell membranes followed by eventual cell death [75,76]. Further, in order to validate the antimicrobial activity, Live/Dead staining of S. aureus and E. coli was employed (Fig. S14 in SI). SYTO 9 and propidium iodide (PI) co-stain was used to stain bacterial cells incubation with CAR3UCD-C film for 24 h (the stain PI and SYTO 9 exhibits a distinct red and green fluorescence that indicates dead cells and live cells respectively) [77]. The fluorescence micrographs of E. coli displayed the high degree of red fluorescence after incubating with CAR3UCD-C as compared with S. aureus (symbolic for the presence of dead cells).

The results observed suggests that UCDs embedded carrageenan films are more effective against Gram-negative bacteria than Gram-positive bacteria. This intriguing outcome may be attributed to the differences in the bacterial cell wall composition, with Gram-positive bacteria having a thicker hydrophilic peptidoglycan layer compared to Gram-negative bacteria. Hydrophobic nature of the carbon dots (UCDs) enable it to attack the lipid outer layer and thin peptidoglycan layer containing Gram-negative bacteria more efficiently.

Biofilm inhibition study is extremely important for food packaging applications since biofilms can contaminate food items and lead to food borne diseases. Biofilms are collections of bacteria that stick to surfaces and can develop on surfaces upon contact with food particles in packaging. The biomass in the treated and control wells were measured by using the Crystal Violet (CV) dye, which is able to stain proteins, carbohydrates, and other biological components. The quantitative assessment of the biofilm production assessed after treatment with CAR3UCD-C showed inhibition of about 62 % and 43 % against E. coli and S. aureus respectively (Fig. 13(c)- (i)-(iv)). The results were corroborated from SEM as depicted in Fig. 13(c)- (v) wherein biofilm growth inhibition was clearly observed along with substantial reduction of initial stage of biofilm growth on the surface. Upon treatment with CAR3UCD and CAR3UCD-C, significant disruption of cell wall integrity and crumpled structural orientation in biofilm growth pattern were observed. This is attributed to the presence of UCDs which help in penetrating the biofilm and kill the bacteria embedded within and the ability of carrageenan in disrupting the biofilm's extracellular matrix [78,79]. This dual synergism, ie mechanical disruption from the polysaccharide carrageenan and oxidative damage from carbon dots ensures a more comprehensive inhibition of biofilm growth.

Evaluating the biocompatibility of films is crucial for ensuring their safety in food packaging applications, as they should not pose any risk to human health when come in direct contact with food items. The MTT assay reveals that the fibroblast cells (L929) sufficiently proliferated and no cytotoxicity was noticed after treatment with the exudates from carrageenan composite films as compared to the control cells (Fig. S15 in SI). Significant difference (p ≤ 0.05) from control was noticed only at 4 % UCD concentration in film. The results are in well accordance with the coated CARUCD sample results as shown in the bar diagram. The tuned cell viability was optimized with CAR3UCD (cell viability >90 %) with no cytotoxicity and further studies carried out with the optimized one [80]. The aforementioned findings were further validated using cytomorphological analysis through SEM micrographs. As shown in the Fig. 13(d), treatment with CAR3UCD and CAR3UCD-C film samples did not cause any adverse cellular responses such as swelling, cell death, or significant changes in cellular architecture when compared to control cells. This indicates the non-toxicity of the carbon dots embedded in carrageenan films, making them suitable for various functional packaging applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of carrageenan based intelligent film for monitoring food freshness
ce:section.ce:para: In view of the ammonia sensing study, the developed intelligent films were applied to check the realtime monitoring of prawns freshness. Prawns was stored under two different temperature condition, 30 °C and 4 °C in containers with intelligent sensing films attached as an observation window as shown in Fig. S16 in SI. The images of films captured using mobile camera under 365 nm light was used to monitor the prawns freshness. Film color changed from orange to blue with time along with the increase in degree of spoilage (Fig. 14(a)). The fluorescence response of film towards volatile amines produced during spoilage was quantified using the change happened to R/B value of the captured images. Total volatile basic nitrogen (TVBN) values were measured parallelly to quantify the extent of spoilage and correlate the color change of film with the real time freshness status. Fresh prawns had a TVBN content of 7.87 mg/100 g and the film exhibited an R/B value of ∼1.5. TVBN value started to increase gradually (p ≤ 0.05) with storage time due to spoilage, which resulted in a simultaneous significant decrease in the R/B value of films (p ≤ 0.05) as shown in Fig. 14(b). TVBN value reached ∼42 mg/100 g by 8 days when stored at 4 °C and the R/B value reached <0.8. As a food item, the maximum acceptable TVBN content in prawns 30 mg/100 g [81]. The data obtained clearly indicates that the developed system demonstrates a significant change in color, along with a corresponding change in the R/B value, before reaching the spoilage point. This gradual change in the R/B value helps to monitor the extent of spoilage effectively. Spoilage was monitored at ambient temperature conditions as well. The spoilage occurred quickly at room temperature conditions (30–35 °C) and food started to rot, resulting in film fluorescence color change within a day, as shown in Fig. 14(c). Thus, the CAR3UCD-C film developed in this study has the potential to function as an indicator label, facilitating real-time, sensitive, and visual monitoring of prawns freshness.

Compostability studies were carried out in natural soil to check the envirinment friendliness of developed films which will greatly benefit their usage as disposable packaging materials [82]. CAR, CAR3UCD and CAR3UCD-C films were burried in soil and the weightloss was monitored periodically for analysing degradation. As shown in Fig. 14(d), the appearance of films changed gradually with time upon degradation in soil for 9, 18 and 27 days under the action of microorganisms and resulted in weight loss (Fig. 14(e)) of films. At the end of 27th day, CAR, CAR3UCD and CAR3UCD-C films exhibited significant weight losses (p ≤ 0.05) of 78.1, 82.34 and 78.64 % respectively. After 35 days, films appeared to be indistinguishable from soil particles as it degrade completely. Carrageenan is a hydrophilic natural biopolymer that rapidly degrades in soil. The coated layer contain zein protein and stearic acid, both of which are naturally occurring components with the ability to get degraded in the environment via bacterial action [83,84]. Since the components are degradable, films developed also shows the potential to be directly degraded and digested by the micro-organisms present in the natural soil.

Urea-dithiosalycilic acid derived carbon dots (UCDs) with ∼10 nm size are synthesised and incorporated in carrageenan biopolymer matrix to develop an intelligent packaging material. Carrageenan-UCDs composite film showed amine responsive feature through change in fluorescent emission color from orange to blue and also showed enhanced moisture barrier property (∼84 % reduction in WVTR) when coated with food safe zein- stearic acid (Z/SA) mixture. The optimized film, CAR3UCD-C exhibited excellent mechanical properties (tensile strength >50 MPa), antioxidant property against ABTS free radicals (70 %) and escalated antibacterial activity against E. coli and S. aureus. The film absorbed nearly all of the ultraviolet (UV) radiation it came into contact with (5.37 % transmittance at 280 nm) and exhibited a cell viability of >90 % in MTT assay. Developed film exhibited home compostable potential by undergoing complete degradation within 35 days of burial in soil. Real time sensing applicability of the film was tested using prawns freshness monitoring.

This study demonstrates the feasibility of using carrageenan-UCDs system for amine sensing application and highlights the ability of zein/SA mixture to act as a moisture barrier coating. The process is scalable because the reagents used are easily available and the solvents are eco-friendly in nature. As solvent casting process is not commercially viable, there is a need to explore melt blown film extrusion process to develop such intelligent film to make it industrially viable, cost effective, less time consuming and energy efficient. The quantity of packaged meat relative to the packaging volume should be maintained as reported in the work to obtain an accurate quantitative sensing performance. In the future, a smart phone app is to be developed to detect the fluorescent color change in the film in response to the amine exposure for real time applications.
